A Mother’s Crusade to Rid South Africa of African Immigrants
Over the past year in South Africa, I have heard several stories about anti-immigrant activists blocking foreigners from entering public health facilities. So I went to check it out for myself.
What struck me at the clinic I visited was that the debate between foreigners and the anti-immigrant activists centered on what it means to be South African today.
The anti-immigrant activists were turning away people born in South Africa because their parents were from other African nations. They didn't care if the person was in the country legally or not.
I spent several months with one of the activists to understand her arguments. She represents many South Africans who feel immigration is the source of their misery.
They have turned their grievances into a national anti-immigration movement in South Africa, which is also drawing fierce criticism from other African nations.
3 Arguments Fueling Anti-Immigrant Sentiment in South Africa
Can Chez Panisse Change With the Times? Does It Need To?

It had been a few years since my last meal at Chez Panisse in Berkeley, one of the most influential restaurants in the country, and I was curious to revisit when I heard it was expanding with a new spot next door.
Alice Waters opened the original restaurant in 1971. It transformed American food, though its goat cheese soufflés can seem sweetly old-fashioned now when you run into them on a four-course set menu.

Bar Panisse works with the same ingredients, though in some ways, it’s less technically ambitious (it’s in the name, it’s a bar!). The room is spare and casual, the menu is short, and plates tend to have very few components.

But I found all this worked to bring it closer to the kind of Mediterranean home cooking that Chez Panisse once idealized. And in its simplicity, spontaneity and energy, it feels remarkably true to the original vision and spirit.
Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
